cards.front: What is an execution path in a C++ program?
cards.back: The specific sequence of statements that the CPU executes when running a program.
A program that takes the same execution path (executes the same statements in the same order) every time it is run.

cards.text: {{c1::Control flow statements}} (also called {{c2::flow control statements}}) allow the programmer to change the normal path of execution through the program.

cards.front: What is branching in the context of control flow?
cards.back: When a control flow statement causes the point of execution to change to a non-sequential statement.

cards.text: The six categories of flow control in C++ are: {{c1::conditional statements}}, {{c2::jumps}}, {{c3::function calls}}, {{c4::loops}}, {{c5::halts}}, and {{c6::exceptions}}.

cards.explanation:
C++ provides six categories of flow control:
  • Conditional statements: Execute code only if a condition is met (if, else, switch)
  • Jumps: Start executing at another location (goto, break, continue)
  • Function calls: Jump to another location and back
  • Loops: Repeatedly execute code (while, do-while, for, ranged-for)
  • Halts: Terminate the program (std::exit(), std::abort())
  • Exceptions: Error handling (try, throw, catch)

cards.text: Conditional statements in C++ are implemented by {{c1::if}}, {{c2::else}}, and {{c3::switch}}.
Jump statements in C++ are {{c1::goto}}, {{c2::break}}, and {{c3::continue}}.
The four loop types in C++ are {{c1::while}}, {{c2::do-while}}, {{c3::for}}, and {{c4::ranged-for}} loops.
To terminate a program early, you can use {{c1::std::exit()}} or {{c2::std::abort()}}.
Exception handling in C++ uses {{c1::try}}, {{c2::throw}}, and {{c3::catch}}.

cards.front: Why are control flow statements important in programming?
cards.back: They allow programs to respond to different conditions and user input dynamically, rather than executing the same sequence every time. Without them, you're restricted to straight-line programs that can't handle variable input or conditions.
